result:
Mutable row — single UPDATE balance = balance - amount WHERE id = ? AND balance >= ?. Atomic via row lock. Fast reads. Matches your wallet code, so reviewers already know the shape.

Cost of mutable — history is lost unless you add a separate audit log. If a redemption is disputed ("card said $50, only $30 left"), you have no trail. You'll end up building a ledger anyway, just bolted on.

Ledger — every issue / redeem / refund / adjust is an insert. Balance = SUM(amount) WHERE card_id = ?. Never updates, only appends. Immutable by construction.

Ledger wins — full audit trail for free, partial refunds trivially modeled as negative-redeem rows, adjustments (customer service credits $5 back) are first-class, finance can reconcile any point in time.

Ledger costs — read is a SUM not a column fetch. Fine up to millions of rows if indexed on card_id; beyond that you cache or snapshot. Concurrency needs an idempotency key + serializable isolation or a per-card advisory lock, because two concurrent inserts can both read balance=$50 and both decide redeem $40 is fine.

Gift cards differ from wallets — wallets are account-scoped, usually one actor, internal money. Gift cards are bearer instruments, touched by finance, legally regulated, often disputed, and auditors will ask for a transaction history. The "we need history eventually" probability is ~100%.

Hybrid — ledger as source of truth, cached balance column on card row updated in same transaction as insert. Reads hit the column, writes append + update. Reconcile job nightly: assert column == SUM(ledger). Best of both, only real cost is remembering to update both in one tx.

Consistency with wallets — not a strong argument. Wallets and gift cards are different domains with different audit requirements. You can keep wallets mutable and make gift cards ledger without anyone getting confused; the schemas already have different tables.

Recommendation — ledger with cached balance. If you want to skip the cache initially, pure ledger is fine at your likely volume; add the cache when a SUM query shows up in slow logs.

One trap — refunds. In mutable model, a refund is balance += amount, and you've just lost the ability to answer "was this card ever used on order X". In ledger, refund row references the original redeem row, and the story is self-evident.
